title: AD688 NLP Analysis - Clean Version
text:
import subprocess, sys
for pkg in ["gdown", "plotly", "kaleido"]:
    subprocess.check_call([sys.executable, "-m", "pip", "install", "-q", pkg])

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from collections import Counter
import warnings, gdown, os
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", 60)
print("Ready.")
# ── Download & clean (self-contained) ────────────────────────────────────────
RAW = "lightcast_2024.csv"
if not os.path.exists(RAW):
    gdown.download("https://drive.google.com/uc?id=1V2GCHGt2dkFGqVBeoUFckU4IhUgk4ocQ",
                   RAW, quiet=False)

df_raw = pd.read_csv(RAW, low_memory=False)
print(f"Raw: {df_raw.shape}")

df = df_raw.copy()

# 1. Drop internal tracking columns
drop1 = ["ID","URL","ACTIVE_URLS","ACTIVE_SOURCES_INFO","DUPLICATES",
         "LAST_UPDATED_TIMESTAMP","LAST_UPDATED_DATE",
         "NAICS2","NAICS3","NAICS4","NAICS5","NAICS6",
         "SOC_2","SOC_2_NAME","SOC_3","SOC_3_NAME","SOC_5","SOC_5_NAME"]
df.drop(columns=[c for c in drop1 if c in df.columns], inplace=True)

# 2. Drop columns >50% missing — protect salary & education columns
PROTECT = ["SALARY","SALARY_FROM","SALARY_TO","ORIGINAL_PAY_PERIOD",
           "MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE","MAX_YEARS_EXPERIENCE",
           "MIN_EDULEVELS","MIN_EDULEVELS_NAME",
           "MAX_EDULEVELS","MAX_EDULEVELS_NAME",
           "LIGHTCAST_SECTORS","LIGHTCAST_SECTORS_NAME"]
prot = [c for c in PROTECT if c in df.columns]
nonp = [c for c in df.columns if c not in prot]
keep = [c for c in nonp if df[c].isnull().mean() < 0.5] + prot
df   = df[keep]

# 3. Deduplicate on key identity columns
dup_key = [c for c in ["TITLE_RAW","COMPANY_NAME","STATE_NAME","POSTED"] if c in df.columns]
df.drop_duplicates(subset=dup_key, keep="first", inplace=True)

# 4. Compute midpoint salary
if "SALARY_FROM" in df.columns and "SALARY_TO" in df.columns:
    df["SALARY_MID"] = df[["SALARY_FROM","SALARY_TO"]].mean(axis=1)
    if "SALARY" in df.columns:
        df["SALARY_MID"] = df["SALARY_MID"].fillna(df["SALARY"])
elif "SALARY" in df.columns:
    df["SALARY_MID"] = df["SALARY"]

# 5. Fill categorical columns
cat_fill = ["NAICS_2022_6_NAME","NAICS_2022_2_NAME","SOC_2021_4_NAME",
            "REMOTE_TYPE_NAME","STATE_NAME","MIN_EDULEVELS_NAME",
            "LOT_V6_OCCUPATION_NAME","LOT_V6_SPECIALIZED_OCCUPATION_NAME",
            "EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME"]
for c in cat_fill:
    if c in df.columns:
        df[c].fillna("Unknown", inplace=True)

# 6. Date parsing
df["POSTED_DT"]    = pd.to_datetime(df["POSTED"], errors="coerce")
df["POSTED_MONTH"] = df["POSTED_DT"].dt.to_period("M").astype(str)
df["POSTED_WEEK"]  = df["POSTED_DT"].dt.to_period("W").astype(str)

print(f"Cleaned: {df.shape}")
print(f"Date range: {df['POSTED_DT'].min().date()} → {df['POSTED_DT'].max().date()}")
print(f"Columns kept: {df.shape[1]}")

title: AD688 NLP Analysis - Clean Version
text:
Section A - AI Tier Classification (Topic 2.1)
##Strategy: 3-tier label built from Lightcast’s own taxonomy + strict title keywords + strict ML tool skills.
## Tiers: AI/ML Core → Advanced Analytics / Engineering → Traditional Analytics / BI

# ── A1. Build 3-tier classifier ───────────────────────────────────────────────

# Keywords for LOT occupation label matching
AI_OCC_KW  = ["machine learning","data scien","ai ","artificial intelligence",
               "deep learning","nlp","computer vision","research scientist",
               "mlops","generative"]
ADV_OCC_KW = ["data engineer","analytics engineer","quantitative",
               "statistician","modeler","architect","computer systems"]

# Strict title keywords (unambiguous AI/ML only)
AI_TITLE_STRICT = [
    "machine learning engineer","ml engineer","data scientist",
    "ai engineer","artificial intelligence","deep learning",
    "nlp engineer","computer vision","research scientist",
    "generative ai","gen ai","llm","mlops","ml ops",
    "applied scientist","ai architect","ml architect",
]
ADV_TITLE_KW = [
    "data engineer","analytics engineer","platform engineer",
    "data architect","solutions architect","enterprise architect",
    "data modeler","quantitative analyst","statistician",
    "data infrastructure","data platform",
]

# Strict ML tool skills (only specific frameworks, not generic data skills)
AI_SKILLS_STRICT = [
    "tensorflow","pytorch","keras","scikit-learn","sklearn",
    "hugging face","langchain","llm","transformers","bert",
    "mlflow","kubeflow","sagemaker","vertex ai","azure machine learning",
    "spark ml","xgboost","lightgbm","catboost",
    "computer vision","natural language processing","reinforcement learning",
    "neural network","deep learning",
]

def classify_by_occ(occ):
    t = str(occ).lower()
    if any(k in t for k in AI_OCC_KW):  return "AI/ML Core"
    if any(k in t for k in ADV_OCC_KW): return "Advanced Analytics / Engineering"
    return "Traditional Analytics / BI"

def classify_by_title(title):
    t = str(title).lower()
    if any(k in t for k in AI_TITLE_STRICT): return "AI/ML Core"
    if any(k in t for k in ADV_TITLE_KW):   return "Advanced Analytics / Engineering"
    return "Traditional Analytics / BI"

def has_ai_skill(skills_str):
    s = str(skills_str).lower()
    return any(k in s for k in AI_SKILLS_STRICT)

# Apply classifiers
occ_col = next((c for c in ["LOT_V6_SPECIALIZED_OCCUPATION_NAME","LOT_V6_OCCUPATION_NAME"]
                if c in df.columns), None)
if occ_col:
    df["TIER_LOT"] = df[occ_col].apply(classify_by_occ)

if "TITLE_RAW" in df.columns:
    df["TIER_TITLE"] = df["TITLE_RAW"].apply(classify_by_title)

if "SKILLS_NAME" in df.columns:
    df["HAS_AI_SKILL"] = df["SKILLS_NAME"].apply(has_ai_skill)

# Combined: LOT primary → title fallback → else Traditional
def combine_tiers(row):
    lot   = row.get("TIER_LOT",   "Traditional Analytics / BI")
    title = row.get("TIER_TITLE", "Traditional Analytics / BI")
    if lot == "AI/ML Core" or title == "AI/ML Core":         return "AI/ML Core"
    if lot == "Advanced Analytics / Engineering" or \
       title == "Advanced Analytics / Engineering":          return "Advanced Analytics / Engineering"
    return "Traditional Analytics / BI"

df["AI_TIER"] = df.apply(combine_tiers, axis=1)

print("AI_TIER distribution:")
vc = df["AI_TIER"].value_counts()
for tier, cnt in vc.items():
    print(f"  {tier:<45s} {cnt:>7,}  ({cnt/len(df)*100:.1f}%)")

print(f"\nAI/ML Core jobs: {(df['AI_TIER']=='AI/ML Core').sum():,}")
if 'HAS_AI_SKILL' in df.columns:
    print(f"Jobs with AI/ML tool skills: {df['HAS_AI_SKILL'].sum():,} ({df['HAS_AI_SKILL'].mean()*100:.1f}%)")


# ── A2. Tier distribution — pie + count bar ───────────────────────────────────
COLOR_MAP = {
    "AI/ML Core":                      "#1565C0",
    "Advanced Analytics / Engineering": "#00897B",
    "Traditional Analytics / BI":       "#78909C",
}

tier_counts = df["AI_TIER"].value_counts().reset_index()
tier_counts.columns = ["Tier", "Count"]

fig = px.pie(
    tier_counts, names="Tier", values="Count", hole=0.45,
    title="Job Distribution by AI Tier (3-Tier Classification)",
    color="Tier", color_discrete_map=COLOR_MAP,
)
fig.update_traces(textinfo="percent+label", textfont_size=13)
fig.update_layout(legend_title_text="Tier", height=450)
fig.show()


# ── A3. Salary comparison by tier ─────────────────────────────────────────────
if "SALARY_MID" in df.columns:
    df_s = df[(df["SALARY_MID"] > 10_000) & (df["SALARY_MID"] < 500_000)].copy()

    fig = px.box(
        df_s, x="AI_TIER", y="SALARY_MID", color="AI_TIER",
        title="Annual Salary by AI Tier",
        labels={"AI_TIER": "Tier", "SALARY_MID": "Annual Salary ($)"},
        color_discrete_map=COLOR_MAP,
    )
    fig.update_layout(showlegend=False, height=450)
    fig.show()

    print("Salary summary by tier:")
    print(df_s.groupby("AI_TIER")["SALARY_MID"]
          .agg(Median="median", Mean="mean", P25=lambda x: x.quantile(0.25),
               P75=lambda x: x.quantile(0.75), Count="count")
          .sort_values("Median", ascending=False)
          .round(0)
          .to_string())
else:
    print("SALARY_MID not available.")


# ── A4. Remote work arrangement by tier ───────────────────────────────────────
if "REMOTE_TYPE_NAME" in df.columns:
    r = df.groupby(["AI_TIER","REMOTE_TYPE_NAME"]).size().reset_index(name="Count")
    r["Share"] = r.groupby("AI_TIER")["Count"].transform(lambda x: x / x.sum() * 100)

    # Simplify labels
    remap = {"Not Remote": "On-Site", "Remote": "Fully Remote",
             "Hybrid Remote": "Hybrid", "Unknown": "Unknown", "[None]": "Not Specified"}
    r["REMOTE_TYPE_NAME"] = r["REMOTE_TYPE_NAME"].map(lambda x: remap.get(x, x))

    fig = px.bar(
        r, x="AI_TIER", y="Share", color="REMOTE_TYPE_NAME",
        barmode="stack",
        title="Work Arrangement by AI Tier (%)",
        labels={"Share": "Share (%)", "AI_TIER": "Tier", "REMOTE_TYPE_NAME": "Work Type"},
        color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set2,
    )
    fig.update_layout(height=420)
    fig.show()

    print("\nRemote work shares:")
    print(r.pivot_table(index="REMOTE_TYPE_NAME", columns="AI_TIER",
                        values="Share", aggfunc="sum").round(1).to_string())


# ── A5. Education requirements by tier ────────────────────────────────────────
edu_col = next((c for c in ["MIN_EDULEVELS_NAME","EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME"] if c in df.columns), None)
if edu_col:
    edu = df.groupby(["AI_TIER", edu_col]).size().reset_index(name="Count")
    edu["Share"] = edu.groupby("AI_TIER")["Count"].transform(lambda x: x / x.sum() * 100)

    fig = px.bar(
        edu, x="AI_TIER", y="Share", color=edu_col,
        barmode="stack",
        title="Minimum Education Requirement by AI Tier (%)",
        labels={"Share": "Share (%)", "AI_TIER": "Tier", edu_col: "Education Level"},
        color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Pastel,
    )
    fig.update_layout(height=420)
    fig.show()
else:
    print("Education column not found.")


# ── A6. Top 15 job titles per tier ────────────────────────────────────────────
if "TITLE_RAW" in df.columns:
    for tier in ["AI/ML Core", "Advanced Analytics / Engineering", "Traditional Analytics / BI"]:
        top = (df[df["AI_TIER"] == tier]["TITLE_RAW"]
               .value_counts().head(15).reset_index())
        top.columns = ["Title", "Count"]
        fig = px.bar(
            top, x="Count", y="Title", orientation="h",
            title=f"Top 15 Job Titles — {tier}",
            color_discrete_sequence=[COLOR_MAP[tier]],
        )
        fig.update_layout(yaxis={"categoryorder": "total ascending"}, height=480)
        fig.show()


# ── A7. Skill share heatmap: tier × top 25 skills ─────────────────────────────
if "SKILLS_NAME" in df.columns:
    def skill_counter(series):
        c = Counter()
        for v in series.dropna():
            s = str(v)
            sep = next((x for x in [";",",","|"] if x in s), None)
            c.update([x.strip() for x in s.split(sep)] if sep else [s.strip()])
        return c

    all_c  = skill_counter(df["SKILLS_NAME"])
    top25  = [sk for sk, _ in all_c.most_common(25)]

    rows = []
    for tier in ["AI/ML Core", "Advanced Analytics / Engineering", "Traditional Analytics / BI"]:
        c     = skill_counter(df[df["AI_TIER"] == tier]["SKILLS_NAME"])
        total = sum(c.values()) or 1
        rows.append({"Tier": tier, **{sk: round(c[sk] / total * 100, 2) for sk in top25}})

    heat = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index("Tier")[top25]
    fig  = px.imshow(
        heat,
        title="Skill Share (%) by AI Tier — Top 25 Skills Overall",
        color_continuous_scale="Blues",
        labels={"color": "%"},
        text_auto=".1f",
        aspect="auto",
    )
    fig.update_layout(height=320)
    fig.show()

title: AD688 NLP Analysis - Clean Version
section: Section B - AI/ML Core Deep Dive
text:
Section B - AI/ML Core Deep Dive
#True AI/ML Core jobs represent only ~0.4% of data/tech postings.
#This section examines where they are, whether they’re growing, and which industries host them.

# ── B0. Subset: AI/ML Core jobs ───────────────────────────────────────────────
ai_core = df[df["AI_TIER"] == "AI/ML Core"].copy()
print(f"AI/ML Core jobs: {len(ai_core):,}")
print(f"Share of total:  {len(ai_core)/len(df)*100:.2f}%")
print(f"\nDate range: {ai_core['POSTED_DT'].min().date()} → {ai_core['POSTED_DT'].max().date()}")

# How were they classified?
if "TIER_LOT" in ai_core.columns and "TIER_TITLE" in ai_core.columns:
    lot_flag   = (ai_core["TIER_LOT"]   == "AI/ML Core").sum()
    title_flag = (ai_core["TIER_TITLE"] == "AI/ML Core").sum()
    print(f"\nFlagged by LOT taxonomy:    {lot_flag:,}")
    print(f"Flagged by title keywords:  {title_flag:,}")


# ── B1. AI/ML Core — monthly posting trend ────────────────────────────────────
ai_monthly = (ai_core[ai_core["POSTED_MONTH"] != "NaT"]
              .groupby("POSTED_MONTH").size().reset_index(name="Postings"))
all_monthly = (df[df["POSTED_MONTH"] != "NaT"]
               .groupby("POSTED_MONTH").size().reset_index(name="Total"))
trend = ai_monthly.merge(all_monthly, on="POSTED_MONTH")
trend["AI_Share_pct"] = trend["Postings"] / trend["Total"] * 100

fig = px.bar(
    trend, x="POSTED_MONTH", y="Postings",
    title="AI/ML Core Job Postings — Monthly Volume (2024)",
    labels={"POSTED_MONTH": "Month", "Postings": "Posting Count"},
    color_discrete_sequence=["#1565C0"],
    text_auto=True,
)
fig.update_layout(height=420)
fig.show()

fig2 = px.line(
    trend, x="POSTED_MONTH", y="AI_Share_pct",
    title="AI/ML Core Share of All Postings (%) — Monthly",
    labels={"POSTED_MONTH": "Month", "AI_Share_pct": "AI/ML Core Share (%)"},
    markers=True,
    color_discrete_sequence=["#1565C0"],
)
fig2.update_layout(height=380)
fig2.show()

print(trend[["POSTED_MONTH","Postings","Total","AI_Share_pct"]].to_string(index=False))


# ── B2. AI/ML Core — state distribution ───────────────────────────────────────
if "STATE_NAME" in ai_core.columns:
    state_counts = ai_core["STATE_NAME"].value_counts().head(20).reset_index()
    state_counts.columns = ["State", "Count"]

    fig = px.bar(
        state_counts, x="Count", y="State", orientation="h",
        title="Top 20 States — AI/ML Core Job Postings",
        color="Count", color_continuous_scale="Blues",
        labels={"Count": "Job Postings", "State": ""},
    )
    fig.update_layout(yaxis={"categoryorder": "total ascending"}, height=540)
    fig.show()

    # Share vs all jobs per state
    all_state = df["STATE_NAME"].value_counts().rename("Total")
    ai_state  = ai_core["STATE_NAME"].value_counts().rename("AI_Core")
    state_df  = pd.concat([ai_state, all_state], axis=1).dropna()
    state_df["AI_Share_pct"] = state_df["AI_Core"] / state_df["Total"] * 100
    state_df = state_df[state_df["Total"] >= 100].sort_values("AI_Share_pct", ascending=False).head(20)

    fig2 = px.bar(
        state_df.reset_index().rename(columns={"STATE_NAME":"State"}),
        x="AI_Share_pct", y="State", orientation="h",
        title="States with Highest AI/ML Core Concentration (%, min 100 postings)",
        color="AI_Share_pct", color_continuous_scale="Blues",
        labels={"AI_Share_pct": "AI/ML Core Share (%)"},
    )
    fig2.update_layout(yaxis={"categoryorder": "total ascending"}, height=540)
    fig2.show()


# ── B3. AI/ML Core — industry breakdown ───────────────────────────────────────
if "NAICS_2022_2_NAME" in ai_core.columns:
    ind_ai = ai_core["NAICS_2022_2_NAME"].value_counts().reset_index()
    ind_ai.columns = ["Industry", "Count"]

    fig = px.pie(
        ind_ai.head(10), names="Industry", values="Count", hole=0.4,
        title="AI/ML Core Jobs by Industry (Top 10)",
        color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set3,
    )
    fig.update_traces(textinfo="percent+label")
    fig.update_layout(height=480)
    fig.show()

    # AI/ML Core concentration ratio per industry
    all_ind   = df["NAICS_2022_2_NAME"].value_counts().rename("Total")
    ai_ind    = ai_core["NAICS_2022_2_NAME"].value_counts().rename("AI_Core")
    ind_share = pd.concat([ai_ind, all_ind], axis=1).dropna()
    ind_share["AI_Share_pct"] = ind_share["AI_Core"] / ind_share["Total"] * 100
    ind_share = ind_share[ind_share["Total"] >= 200].sort_values("AI_Share_pct", ascending=False)

    fig2 = px.bar(
        ind_share.reset_index().rename(columns={"NAICS_2022_2_NAME": "Industry"}),
        x="AI_Share_pct", y="Industry", orientation="h",
        title="AI/ML Core Concentration by Industry (%, min 200 total postings)",
        color="AI_Share_pct", color_continuous_scale="Blues",
        labels={"AI_Share_pct": "AI/ML Core Share (%)"},
    )
    fig2.update_layout(yaxis={"categoryorder": "total ascending"}, height=500)
    fig2.show()


# ── B4. AI/ML Core — top companies and top skills ─────────────────────────────
if "COMPANY_NAME" in ai_core.columns:
    top_co = ai_core["COMPANY_NAME"].value_counts().head(15).reset_index()
    top_co.columns = ["Company", "Count"]
    fig = px.bar(
        top_co, x="Count", y="Company", orientation="h",
        title="Top 15 Companies Hiring for AI/ML Core Roles",
        color_discrete_sequence=["#1565C0"],
    )
    fig.update_layout(yaxis={"categoryorder": "total ascending"}, height=500)
    fig.show()

if "SKILLS_NAME" in ai_core.columns:
    def skill_counter(series):
        c = Counter()
        for v in series.dropna():
            s = str(v)
            sep = next((x for x in [";",",","|"] if x in s), None)
            c.update([x.strip() for x in s.split(sep)] if sep else [s.strip()])
        return c

    ai_skills = skill_counter(ai_core["SKILLS_NAME"])
    top_skills = pd.DataFrame(ai_skills.most_common(25), columns=["Skill", "Count"])

    fig = px.bar(
        top_skills, x="Count", y="Skill", orientation="h",
        title="Top 25 Skills in AI/ML Core Jobs",
        color_discrete_sequence=["#1565C0"],
    )
    fig.update_layout(yaxis={"categoryorder": "total ascending"}, height=580)
    fig.show()


# ── B5. AI/ML Core — salary distribution and remote work ──────────────────────
if "SALARY_MID" in ai_core.columns:
    ai_sal = ai_core[(ai_core["SALARY_MID"] > 10_000) & (ai_core["SALARY_MID"] < 500_000)]
    print(f"AI/ML Core jobs with salary data: {len(ai_sal):,}")
    print(f"  Median salary: ${ai_sal['SALARY_MID'].median():,.0f}")
    print(f"  Mean salary:   ${ai_sal['SALARY_MID'].mean():,.0f}")
    print(f"  25th pct:      ${ai_sal['SALARY_MID'].quantile(0.25):,.0f}")
    print(f"  75th pct:      ${ai_sal['SALARY_MID'].quantile(0.75):,.0f}")

    fig = px.histogram(
        ai_sal, x="SALARY_MID", nbins=30,
        title="AI/ML Core — Salary Distribution",
        labels={"SALARY_MID": "Annual Salary ($)"},
        color_discrete_sequence=["#1565C0"],
    )
    fig.update_layout(height=380)
    fig.show()

if "REMOTE_TYPE_NAME" in ai_core.columns:
    remote_ai = ai_core["REMOTE_TYPE_NAME"].value_counts().reset_index()
    remote_ai.columns = ["Work Type", "Count"]
    fig = px.pie(
        remote_ai, names="Work Type", values="Count",
        title="AI/ML Core — Work Arrangement Breakdown",
        color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set2,
        hole=0.4,
    )
    fig.update_traces(textinfo="percent+label")
    fig.update_layout(height=400)
    fig.show()

title: AD688 NLP Analysis - Clean Version
text:
Section C - Temporal & Industry Trends (Topic 2.5)
#Focus: How is the data/analytics/tech job market evolving across May–September 2024?
#Key dimensions: industry growth, occupation trends, emerging/declining titles, skill demand evolution.

# ── C1. Overall posting volume over time ──────────────────────────────────────
monthly_vol = (df[df["POSTED_MONTH"] != "NaT"]
               .groupby("POSTED_MONTH").size().reset_index(name="Postings"))

fig = px.bar(
    monthly_vol, x="POSTED_MONTH", y="Postings",
    title="Total Monthly Data/Tech Job Posting Volume (2024)",
    labels={"POSTED_MONTH": "Month", "Postings": "Job Postings"},
    color_discrete_sequence=["#37474F"],
    text_auto=True,
)
fig.update_layout(height=400)
fig.show()

print(monthly_vol.to_string(index=False))


# ── C2. Occupation category trends over time ───────────────────────────────────
occ_col = next((c for c in ["LOT_V6_OCCUPATION_NAME","LOT_OCCUPATION_NAME"] if c in df.columns), None)
if occ_col:
    occ_trend = (df[df["POSTED_MONTH"] != "NaT"]
                 .groupby(["POSTED_MONTH", occ_col])
                 .size().reset_index(name="Postings"))

    fig = px.line(
        occ_trend, x="POSTED_MONTH", y="Postings", color=occ_col,
        title="Job Postings by Occupation Category Over Time",
        markers=True,
        labels={"POSTED_MONTH": "Month", occ_col: "Occupation"},
    )
    fig.update_xaxes(tickangle=-30)
    fig.update_layout(height=480)
    fig.show()

    # Growth rate: first month vs. last month
    months = sorted(occ_trend["POSTED_MONTH"].unique())
    if len(months) >= 2:
        first  = occ_trend[occ_trend["POSTED_MONTH"] == months[0]].set_index(occ_col)["Postings"]
        last   = occ_trend[occ_trend["POSTED_MONTH"] == months[-1]].set_index(occ_col)["Postings"]
        growth = ((last - first) / first * 100).sort_values(ascending=False)
        print(f"Occupation posting growth ({months[0]} → {months[-1]}):")
        print(growth.apply(lambda x: f"{x:+.1f}%").to_string())


# ── C3. Industry posting trends over time (top 8) ─────────────────────────────
if "NAICS_2022_2_NAME" in df.columns:
    top_ind = df["NAICS_2022_2_NAME"].value_counts().head(8).index
    ind_trend = (df[df["NAICS_2022_2_NAME"].isin(top_ind) & (df["POSTED_MONTH"] != "NaT")]
                 .groupby(["POSTED_MONTH","NAICS_2022_2_NAME"])
                 .size().reset_index(name="Postings"))

    fig = px.line(
        ind_trend, x="POSTED_MONTH", y="Postings", color="NAICS_2022_2_NAME",
        title="Job Postings by Industry Over Time (Top 8 Industries)",
        markers=True,
        labels={"POSTED_MONTH": "Month", "NAICS_2022_2_NAME": "Industry"},
    )
    fig.update_xaxes(tickangle=-30)
    fig.update_layout(height=500)
    fig.show()

    months = sorted(ind_trend["POSTED_MONTH"].unique())
    if len(months) >= 2:
        first  = ind_trend[ind_trend["POSTED_MONTH"] == months[0]].set_index("NAICS_2022_2_NAME")["Postings"]
        last   = ind_trend[ind_trend["POSTED_MONTH"] == months[-1]].set_index("NAICS_2022_2_NAME")["Postings"]
        growth = ((last - first) / first * 100).sort_values(ascending=False)
        print(f"Industry posting growth ({months[0]} → {months[-1]}):")
        print(growth.apply(lambda x: f"{x:+.1f}%").to_string())


# ── C4. Emerging vs. declining job titles (early vs. late 2024) ───────────────
if "TITLE_RAW" in df.columns and "POSTED_DT" in df.columns:
    mid_date  = df["POSTED_DT"].dropna().median()
    df_early  = df[df["POSTED_DT"] < mid_date]
    df_late   = df[df["POSTED_DT"] >= mid_date]

    early_c = df_early["TITLE_RAW"].value_counts()
    late_c  = df_late["TITLE_RAW"].value_counts()

    common = [t for t in early_c.index.intersection(late_c.index) if early_c[t] >= 20]
    tg = pd.DataFrame({"Early": early_c[common], "Late": late_c[common]}).dropna()
    tg["Growth_pct"] = (tg["Late"] - tg["Early"]) / tg["Early"] * 100

    print(f"Period split: Early < {mid_date.date()} | Late >= {mid_date.date()}")
    print(f"Titles with ≥20 early postings: {len(tg)}")

    top_growing = tg.sort_values("Growth_pct", ascending=False).head(20).reset_index()
    top_growing.columns = ["Title","Early","Late","Growth_pct"]

    fig = px.bar(
        top_growing, x="Growth_pct", y="Title", orientation="h",
        title=f"Top 20 Fastest-Growing Job Titles (Early → Late 2024)",
        labels={"Growth_pct": "Growth (%)", "Title": ""},
        color="Growth_pct", color_continuous_scale="Greens",
    )
    fig.update_layout(yaxis={"categoryorder": "total ascending"}, height=560)
    fig.show()

    top_declining = tg.sort_values("Growth_pct").head(20).reset_index()
    top_declining.columns = ["Title","Early","Late","Growth_pct"]

    fig2 = px.bar(
        top_declining, x="Growth_pct", y="Title", orientation="h",
        title=f"Top 20 Fastest-Declining Job Titles (Early → Late 2024)",
        labels={"Growth_pct": "Growth (%)", "Title": ""},
        color="Growth_pct", color_continuous_scale="Reds_r",
    )
    fig2.update_layout(yaxis={"categoryorder": "total ascending"}, height=560)
    fig2.show()


# ── C5. Skill group demand evolution ──────────────────────────────────────────
if "SKILLS_NAME" in df.columns:
    SKILL_GROUPS = {
        "AI/ML Tools":     ["tensorflow","pytorch","keras","scikit-learn",
                            "hugging face","langchain","mlflow","sagemaker"],
        "Traditional BI":  ["tableau","power bi","excel","qlik","cognos",
                            "crystal reports","microstrategy"],
        "Programming":     ["python","r programming","scala","julia"],
        "Cloud Platforms": ["aws","azure","google cloud","databricks","snowflake"],
        "Core Data Skills":["sql","data analysis","data visualization","statistics"],
    }

    months = sorted(df["POSTED_MONTH"].dropna().astype(str).unique())
    records = []
    for m in months:
        mdf = df[df["POSTED_MONTH"] == m]
        for grp, kws in SKILL_GROUPS.items():
            share = mdf["SKILLS_NAME"].dropna().apply(
                lambda s: any(k in str(s).lower() for k in kws)
            ).mean() * 100
            records.append({"Month": m, "Skill Group": grp, "Share": round(share, 2)})

    skill_trend = pd.DataFrame(records)
    fig = px.line(
        skill_trend, x="Month", y="Share", color="Skill Group",
        title="Skill Group Demand Over Time (% of Postings Mentioning)",
        markers=True,
        labels={"Share": "% of Postings", "Month": ""},
    )
    fig.update_xaxes(tickangle=-30)
    fig.update_layout(height=460)
    fig.show()


# ── C6. AI/ML tool penetration by industry ────────────────────────────────────
if "SKILLS_NAME" in df.columns and "NAICS_2022_2_NAME" in df.columns:
    AI_TOOLS = ["tensorflow","pytorch","keras","scikit-learn",
                "hugging face","langchain","mlflow","sagemaker"]

    df["HAS_AI_TOOL"] = df["SKILLS_NAME"].apply(
        lambda s: any(k in str(s).lower() for k in AI_TOOLS)
    )

    ind_ai = (df.groupby("NAICS_2022_2_NAME")["HAS_AI_TOOL"]
              .agg(AI_pct="mean", Count="count")
              .reset_index()
              .query("Count >= 100"))
    ind_ai["AI_pct"] = (ind_ai["AI_pct"] * 100).round(2)
    ind_ai = ind_ai.sort_values("AI_pct", ascending=False)

    fig = px.bar(
        ind_ai, x="AI_pct", y="NAICS_2022_2_NAME", orientation="h",
        title="% of Postings Requiring AI/ML Tools by Industry",
        labels={"AI_pct": "% with AI/ML Tool Requirement",
                "NAICS_2022_2_NAME": "Industry"},
        color="AI_pct", color_continuous_scale="Blues",
    )
    fig.update_layout(yaxis={"categoryorder": "total ascending"}, height=620)
    fig.show()

    print("Industries with highest AI/ML tool requirement:")
    print(ind_ai[["NAICS_2022_2_NAME","AI_pct","Count"]].head(10).to_string(index=False))


# ── C7. Median salary trend over time ─────────────────────────────────────────
if "SALARY_MID" in df.columns:
    sal_trend = (df[(df["SALARY_MID"] > 10_000) & (df["SALARY_MID"] < 500_000)
                    & (df["POSTED_MONTH"] != "NaT")]
                 .groupby("POSTED_MONTH")["SALARY_MID"]
                 .agg(Median="median", Mean="mean", Count="count")
                 .reset_index())

    fig = px.line(
        sal_trend, x="POSTED_MONTH", y="Median",
        title="Median Salary Over Time — Data/Tech Jobs (2024)",
        markers=True,
        labels={"POSTED_MONTH": "Month", "Median": "Median Annual Salary ($)"},
        color_discrete_sequence=["#37474F"],
    )
    fig.update_xaxes(tickangle=-30)
    fig.update_layout(height=400)
    fig.show()

    # Also: salary by tier over time
    if "AI_TIER" in df.columns:
        sal_tier_trend = (df[(df["SALARY_MID"] > 10_000) & (df["SALARY_MID"] < 500_000)
                             & (df["POSTED_MONTH"] != "NaT")]
                          .groupby(["POSTED_MONTH","AI_TIER"])["SALARY_MID"]
                          .median().reset_index(name="Median_Salary"))

        fig2 = px.line(
            sal_tier_trend, x="POSTED_MONTH", y="Median_Salary", color="AI_TIER",
            title="Median Salary Over Time by AI Tier",
            markers=True,
            labels={"POSTED_MONTH": "Month", "Median_Salary": "Median Salary ($)",
                    "AI_TIER": "Tier"},
            color_discrete_map=COLOR_MAP,
        )
        fig2.update_xaxes(tickangle=-30)
        fig2.update_layout(height=420)
        fig2.show()

    print(sal_trend.to_string(index=False))


# ── C8. Posting volume trend by AI tier (combined view) ───────────────────────
if "AI_TIER" in df.columns:
    tier_trend = (df[df["POSTED_MONTH"] != "NaT"]
                  .groupby(["POSTED_MONTH","AI_TIER"])
                  .size().reset_index(name="Postings"))

    fig = px.line(
        tier_trend, x="POSTED_MONTH", y="Postings", color="AI_TIER",
        title="Posting Volume Trend by AI Tier (2024)",
        markers=True,
        color_discrete_map=COLOR_MAP,
        labels={"POSTED_MONTH": "Month", "AI_TIER": "Tier"},
    )
    fig.update_xaxes(tickangle=-30)
    fig.update_layout(height=440)
    fig.show()

title: AD688 NLP Analysis - Clean Version
text:
Section D - Key Findings Summary

# ── D. Print consolidated findings ────────────────────────────────────────────
sep = "=" * 70
print(sep)
print(" KEY FINDINGS — AD688 Final EDA")
print(sep)

print("""
DATASET OVERVIEW
----------------------------------
- Raw: 72,498 rows × 131 columns  |  Cleaned: 67,572 rows × 117 columns
- Date range: 2024-05-01 → 2024-09-30  (5 months)
- Scope: data / analytics / tech occupations (Lightcast pre-filter)
""")

print("""
TOPIC 2.1 — AI vs. Non-AI Careers
----------------------------------
1. Classification breakdown (3-tier combined classifier):
   - Traditional Analytics / BI        57,870  (85.6%)
   - Advanced Analytics / Engineering   9,398  (13.9%)
   - AI/ML Core                           304   (0.4%)
   → Only 1.8% of postings explicitly list specific ML framework skills
     (TensorFlow, PyTorch, Keras, etc.).
   → All 304 AI/ML Core jobs were flagged by STRICT TITLE KEYWORDS —
     the Lightcast LOT taxonomy flagged ZERO as AI/ML Core,
     confirming the taxonomy is too broad to distinguish true AI roles.

2. Salary gradient (confirmed):
   Advanced Analytics / Engineering   Median ,000  (P25 k  P75 k)
   AI/ML Core                         Median ,000  (P25 $ 97k  P75 k)
   Traditional Analytics / BI         Median ,000  (P25 $ 75k  P75 k)
   → Counter-intuitive: Advanced Analytics (architects, senior data engineers)
     out-earns labeled 'AI/ML Core' roles — likely due to seniority mix.
     The salary premium for AI roles exists but is less extreme than expected.

3. Remote work — SURPRISING finding:
   Fully Remote:  AI/ML Core 6.6%  |  Advanced 25.0%  |  Traditional 16.4%
   On-Site:       AI/ML Core 13.2% |  Advanced  1.1%  |  Traditional  1.6%
   → AI/ML Core roles are the LEAST remote and MOST on-site of the three tiers.
     Advanced Analytics/Engineering is the most remote-friendly tier (25%).
     ~78% of all postings do not specify remote type.
""")

print("""
SECTION B — AI/ML CORE DEEP DIVE
---------------------------------
4. Monthly growth trend (AI/ML Core):
   May 51 → Jun 60 → Jul 60 → Aug 65 → Sep 68  (+33% over 5 months)
   Market share: 0.37% (May) → 0.47% (Sep)
   → Despite tiny absolute numbers, AI/ML Core postings show a consistent
     upward trend throughout the observation window.

5. Geographic distribution (AI/ML Core volume):
   Top states: Texas, California, Virginia, North Carolina, Illinois,
               Maryland, Pennsylvania, New York
   Top by concentration: Maryland, Washington D.C., Connecticut, Alabama,
               Minnesota lead in AI/ML Core share relative to total postings.
   → AI/ML Core is NOT exclusively a coastal/tech-hub phenomenon.

6. Industry distribution (AI/ML Core):
   Highest AI/ML Core CONCENTRATION by industry (min 200 postings):
   Transportation & Warehousing > Mining/Oil & Gas > Information > Finance
   → Surprisingly, Transportation & Warehousing has the highest ML-tool
     requirement rate (1.44%), ahead of Information (0.92%) and Finance (0.90%).
     This likely reflects autonomous vehicle, route optimization, and logistics AI.

7. Top companies hiring AI/ML Core: Honu Services, Capgemini, Penske,
   Deloitte, Disney, Mayo Clinic, Nestlé, Cognizant, US Bank, Travelers
   → Mix of consulting firms, healthcare, automotive, and financial services.

8. Top skills in AI/ML Core: Data Science, Machine Learning, Python,
   Statistics, AI, Mathematics, AWS, Azure, Generative AI, Computer Science
   → Core ML skills + cloud platforms dominate; Generative AI already appears
     in top 25 skills, reflecting 2024 market shift.
""")

print("""
TOPIC 2.5 — BA / Data Science / ML Trends
------------------------------------------
9.  Overall posting volume:
    May 13,897 → Jun 13,159 → Jul 11,904 → Aug 14,221 → Sep 14,390
    → Volume dipped in July (summer slowdown) but recovered strongly by Sep.

10. Occupation growth (May → Sep 2024):
    Business / Management Analyst        +51.4%  (fastest growing)
    Data / Data Mining Analyst           +11.0%
    Clinical Analyst                      -3.4%
    Business Intelligence Analyst         -4.5%
    Computer Systems Engineer/Architect  -11.2%
    Market Research Analyst              -15.0%
    → Demand for generalist business analytics roles surged while
      specialized engineering and BI roles declined.

11. Industry growth (May → Sep 2024):
    Information                           +33.2%
    Finance and Insurance                 +15.2%
    Wholesale Trade                       +14.5%
    Health Care and Social Assistance      +7.7%
    Professional/Scientific/Technical     -1.9%
    Administrative & Support Services     -5.8%
    → Information and Finance are the fastest-growing employers of
      data/tech talent; traditionally dominant PSTS sector is flat.

12. AI/ML tool skill penetration (% of postings, by industry):
    Transportation & Warehousing 1.44% | Mining 1.22% | Information 0.92%
    Finance 0.90% | PSTS 0.67% | Education 0.66%
    → Overall penetration is very low (<2%) and FLAT across the 5-month
      window — AI adoption in job postings was NOT accelerating in 2024H2.

13. Salary trend:
    May ,500 → Jun ,000 → Jul ,200 → Aug ,685 → Sep ,000
    → Salary dipped during summer (typical hiring cycle pattern) then
      partially recovered, ending below the May peak. No clear upward trend
      in the short observation window.
""")

print(sep)

title: AD688 NLP Analysis - Clean Version
section: Section E - NLP Analysis — Job Description Insights
text:
Section E - NLP Analysis — Job Description Insights
#This section extends the analysis beyond structured variables by applying Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques to job descriptions. #The goal is to extract key skills, understand employer requirements, and identify hidden patterns in unstructured text data.

import re
def clean_text(text):
    text = str(text).lower()
    text = re.sub(r'<[^>]+>', ' ', text)   
    text = re.sub(r'[^a-z\s]', ' ', text)  
    text = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', text).strip()
    return text
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer, TfidfVectorizer
text_col = next((c for c in df.columns if "desc" in c.lower()), None)

if text_col is None:
    text_data = df["TITLE_RAW"].dropna().astype(str)
else:
    text_data = df[text_col].apply(clean_text).dropna().astype(str)

text_data = text_data[text_data.str.len() > 2]


vectorizer = CountVectorizer(stop_words='english', max_features=20)
text_data = text_data.dropna().astype(str)
text_data = text_data[text_data.str.len() > 2]
X = vectorizer.fit_transform(text_data)

word_counts = pd.DataFrame({
    "word": vectorizer.get_feature_names_out(),
    "count": X.toarray().sum(axis=0)
}).sort_values(by="count", ascending=False)

word_counts


tfidf = TfidfVectorizer(stop_words='english', max_features=20)
X_tfidf = tfidf.fit_transform(text_data)

tfidf_scores = pd.DataFrame({
    "word": tfidf.get_feature_names_out(),
    "score": X_tfidf.toarray().mean(axis=0)
}).sort_values(by="score", ascending=False)

tfidf_scores


skills = ["python","sql","excel","machine learning","tableau","power bi","communication"]

skill_counts = {s: text_data.str.contains(s).sum() for s in skills}

skill_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "skill": skill_counts.keys(),
    "count": skill_counts.values()
}).sort_values(by="count", ascending=False)

skill_df


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

skill_df_sorted = skill_df.sort_values(by="count")

plt.figure()
plt.barh(skill_df_sorted["skill"], skill_df_sorted["count"])
plt.title("Top Skills in Job Descriptions")
plt.show()


df_salary = df.dropna(subset=["SALARY_MID"])
df_salary["has_python"] = df_salary["TITLE_RAW"].astype(str).str.contains("python", na=False)
df_salary.groupby("has_python")["SALARY_MID"].mean().reset_index()

title: Skill Gap Analysis
text:
This section evaluates the gap between our team’s current technical skill levels and the skills most demanded in the 2024 job market. Building on our Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA), we focus on key skill categories in data science, business analytics, and machine learning roles. The goal is to identify strengths, highlight gaps, and provide actionable recommendations for career development.



Based on our EDA of the Lightcast dataset, job postings in data and analytics roles emphasize the following skill groups:

Programming: Python, R, Scala

Core Data Skills: SQL, statistics, data analysis, data visualization

Traditional BI Tools: Tableau, Power BI, Excel

Cloud Platforms: AWS, Azure, Databricks, Snowflake

AI/ML Tools: TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn, MLflow, SageMaker

These findings indicate that employers increasingly expect candidates to combine strong analytical foundations with technical and cloud-based capabilities.



We evaluated the current skill levels of each team member using a 1–5 scale:

1 = Beginner

2 = Basic Knowledge

3 = Intermediate

4 = Advanced

5 = Expert


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np

skills_data = {
    "Name": ["Zifan Chen", "Jingyuan Yang", "Zhenjie Shao", "Zhuolun Wu", "Almas Tussipov"],
    "Python": [3, 3, 4, 4, 4],
    "SQL": [3, 5, 3, 3, 4],
    "Statistics": [4, 4, 4, 4, 4],
    "Data Visualization": [5, 5, 4, 4, 5],
    "Excel": [4, 5, 4, 4, 5],
    "Tableau/Power BI": [5, 4, 3, 4, 5],
    "AWS/Azure": [3, 3, 3, 3, 3],
    "Machine Learning": [3, 3, 4, 3, 4],
    "AI/ML Tools": [4, 4, 4, 4, 2]
}

df_skills = pd.DataFrame(skills_data)
df_skills = df_skills.set_index("Name")
df_skills

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.heatmap(df_skills, annot=True, cmap="YlGnBu", linewidths=0.5, fmt=".0f")
plt.title("Team Skill Levels Heatmap")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()










The heatmap shows that the team is relatively strong in foundational skills such as Excel, Python, and data visualization. However, advanced skills such as cloud platforms, machine learning, and AI tools are relatively weak across most members.
This suggests that the team is well-prepared for basic data analysis tasks but may face challenges when applying advanced analytics techniques in real-world business environments.




To compare internal capabilities with market expectations, we define a simplified benchmark based on the key skill groups identified in our EDA.

market_demand = pd.Series({
    "Python": 5,
    "SQL": 5,
    "Statistics": 4,
    "Data Visualization": 4,
    "Excel": 3,
    "Tableau/Power BI": 4,
    "AWS/Azure": 4,
    "Machine Learning": 4,
    "AI/ML Tools": 3
}, name="Market Demand")

team_average = df_skills.mean(axis=0).round(2)

gap_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Team Average": team_average,
    "Market Demand": market_demand
})

gap_df["Gap"] = (gap_df["Market Demand"] - gap_df["Team Average"]).round(2)
gap_df = gap_df.sort_values("Gap", ascending=False)

gap_df

Gap

Excel
4.4
3
-1.4












plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.barplot(x=gap_df["Gap"], y=gap_df.index)
plt.title("Skill Gap: Market Demand vs Team Average")
plt.xlabel("Gap Size")
plt.ylabel("Skill")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()










The largest gaps appear in cloud platforms, machine learning, and AI-related tools. The team is relatively closer to market expectations in Excel, Python, and basic data analysis skills.
This indicates that while the team has a solid analytical foundation, there is a need to strengthen more advanced technical capabilities to remain competitive in the job market.




To address the identified gaps, the following actions are recommended:

Strengthen Python and SQL proficiency across all team members
Learn cloud platforms such as AWS or Azure
Develop machine learning skills using libraries like scikit-learn
Improve data visualization skills using Tableau, Power BI, or Plotly
Work on real-world projects to integrate analysis, modelling, and visualization




Overall, the team demonstrates a strong foundation in core analytics skills. However, gaps in advanced technical areas such as cloud computing and machine learning suggest the need for continued skill development.
By aligning learning efforts with market demand, the team can significantly improve its competitiveness in business analytics and data science roles.

text:
To compare internal capabilities with market expectations, we define a simplified benchmark based on the key skill groups identified in our EDA.

market_demand = pd.Series({
    "Python": 5,
    "SQL": 5,
    "Statistics": 4,
    "Data Visualization": 4,
    "Excel": 3,
    "Tableau/Power BI": 4,
    "AWS/Azure": 4,
    "Machine Learning": 4,
    "AI/ML Tools": 3
}, name="Market Demand")

team_average = df_skills.mean(axis=0).round(2)

gap_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Team Average": team_average,
    "Market Demand": market_demand
})

gap_df["Gap"] = (gap_df["Market Demand"] - gap_df["Team Average"]).round(2)
gap_df = gap_df.sort_values("Gap", ascending=False)

title: Skill Gap Analysis
text:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.barplot(x=gap_df["Gap"], y=gap_df.index)
plt.title("Skill Gap: Market Demand vs Team Average")
plt.xlabel("Gap Size")
plt.ylabel("Skill")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()










The largest gaps appear in cloud platforms, machine learning, and AI-related tools. The team is relatively closer to market expectations in Excel, Python, and basic data analysis skills.
This indicates that while the team has a solid analytical foundation, there is a need to strengthen more advanced technical capabilities to remain competitive in the job market.

title: Skill Gap Analysis
text:
To address the identified gaps, the following actions are recommended:

Strengthen Python and SQL proficiency across all team members
Learn cloud platforms such as AWS or Azure
Develop machine learning skills using libraries like scikit-learn
Improve data visualization skills using Tableau, Power BI, or Plotly
Work on real-world projects to integrate analysis, modelling, and visualization

title: Skill Gap Analysis
text:
Overall, the team demonstrates a strong foundation in core analytics skills. However, gaps in advanced technical areas such as cloud computing and machine learning suggest the need for continued skill development.
By aligning learning efforts with market demand, the team can significantly improve its competitiveness in business analytics and data science roles.

title: Business Analytics, Data Science, and Machine Learning Trends
text:
1 Introduction
Over the past decade, the need for professionals in data science, business analytics, and machine learning has expanded significantly (manyika2011?). As organizations increasingly depend on data to guide strategic and operational decisions, understanding current labor market dynamics has become critical for individuals planning careers in these fields.
This project examines job market trends in 2024 using Lightcast job postings data (lightcast2024?). The analysis focuses on several key questions:

Which technical and analytical skills are most frequently required?
What industries demonstrate the highest demand for data-related roles?
How do salaries differ based on geography, experience, and skill sets?
What are the future prospects for careers in business analytics?

By addressing these questions, the study aims to provide a clearer picture of opportunities available to aspiring data professionals.

title: Business Analytics, Data Science, and Machine Learning Trends
text:
2 Research Rationale
The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence and automation technologies has reshaped workforce demands across industries. Roles in data science and analytics are now considered among the most valuable in the modern economy (davenport2012?). According to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (bls2024?), employment in data-related occupations is projected to grow at a rate exceeding the average for most professions.
Given these trends, it is essential for job seekers to understand which competencies are most valued, where opportunities are concentrated, and how compensation varies across different contexts. This study adopts the perspective of an individual entering the data field in 2024 and uses real-world job posting data to identify patterns that can inform career decisions. Literature Review
This analysis takes the perspective of a job seeker entering the data science field in 2024, examining real job posting data to identify trends and opportunities.

title: Business Analytics, Data Science, and Machine Learning Trends
text:
3 Literature Review
Recent studies and industry reports emphasize the evolving requirements of data-related careers. Findings from sources such as the Kaggle Developer Survey indicate that programming languages like Python and SQL continue to dominate job requirements, alongside machine learning tools and frameworks (kaggle2023?; python2024?). In addition, demand for cloud computing skills, including platforms such as AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud, has grown steadily.
Research also highlights regional differences in compensation, with major technology hubs such as San Francisco, Seattle, and New York typically offering higher salaries (linkedin2024?). However, the increasing adoption of remote work has begun to reduce geographic barriers, allowing broader access to opportunities.
Furthermore, business analytics roles are increasingly interdisciplinary, requiring not only technical proficiency but also the ability to interpret data and communicate insights effectively (provost2013?). Employers now prioritize candidates who can connect analytical results to real-world business outcomes.

title: Business Analytics, Data Science, and Machine Learning Trends
